quiz.title: Algebra-2: Unit 1 Quiz 1
quiz.subtitle: Solving Multi-Step Equations and Inequalities
quiz.subject: Algebra 2
quiz.topic: Algebra I Review: 1.1 Solving Multi-Step Equations and Inequalities

questions.question: Solve: $5x - 3(2x - 1) = 4(x + 2) - 7$.
questions.explanation: Distribute: $5x - 6x + 3 = 4x + 8 - 7$. Simplify: $-x + 3 = 4x + 1$. Subtract $4x$ from both sides: $-5x + 3 = 1$. Subtract $3$: $-5x = -2$. $x = \frac{2}{5}$. Check by substituting back into the original equation.

questions.question: Solve: $\frac{2x - 1}{3} - \frac{x + 2}{4} = 1$.
questions.explanation: Multiply by LCD $12$: $4(2x - 1) - 3(x + 2) = 12$. $8x - 4 - 3x - 6 = 12$. $5x - 10 = 12$. $5x = 22$. $x = \frac{22}{5}$. Always clear fractions first by multiplying by the LCD.

questions.question: Solve: $4(2x - 3) = 8x + 5$.
questions.explanation: Distribute: $8x - 12 = 8x + 5$. Subtract $8x$: $-12 = 5$. This is a false statement, so there is no solution. The lines $y = 4(2x - 3)$ and $y = 8x + 5$ are parallel with different $y$-intercepts.

questions.question: Solve: $3(x - 2) + 2x = 5x - 6$.
questions.explanation: $3x - 6 + 2x = 5x - 6$. $5x - 6 = 5x - 6$. Subtract $5x$: $-6 = -6$. This is always true, so every real number is a solution. The two sides are identical expressions — the equation is an identity.

questions.question: Solve the compound inequality: $-3 \leq 2x + 1 < 9$.
questions.explanation: Subtract $1$ throughout: $-4 \leq 2x < 8$. Divide by $2$: $-2 \leq x < 4$. In interval notation: $[-2, 4)$. The left endpoint is included (closed bracket), the right is not (open bracket).

questions.question: Solve the compound inequality: $3x - 5 < -8$ or $4x + 1 \geq 17$.
questions.explanation: First inequality: $3x < -3$, so $x < -1$. Second: $4x \geq 16$, so $x \geq 4$. With 'or', the solution is the union: $(-\infty, -1) \cup [4, \infty)$. 'Or' means a value only needs to satisfy one of the inequalities.

questions.question: Solve the absolute value equation: $|2x - 5| = 11$.
questions.explanation: $2x - 5 = 11$ or $2x - 5 = -11$. First: $2x = 16$, $x = 8$. Second: $2x = -6$, $x = -3$. Absolute value equations of the form $|ax + b| = c$ (with $c > 0$) always yield two solutions.

questions.question: Solve the absolute value inequality: $|x + 4| < 7$.
questions.explanation: $|x + 4| < 7$ means $-7 < x + 4 < 7$. Subtract $4$: $-11 < x < 3$. Interval: $(-11, 3)$. 'Less than' absolute value inequalities produce a single bounded interval.

questions.question: Solve the absolute value inequality: $|3x - 2| \geq 10$.
questions.explanation: $|3x - 2| \geq 10$ splits into $3x - 2 \geq 10$ or $3x - 2 \leq -10$. First: $3x \geq 12$, $x \geq 4$. Second: $3x \leq -8$, $x \leq -\frac{8}{3}$. 'Greater than or equal to' produces two unbounded intervals: $(-\infty, -\frac{8}{3}] \cup [4, \infty)$.

questions.question: A rectangle has length $3x + 2$ and width $x - 1$. The perimeter is $50$ units. Find the length and width.

scoreMessages.90: Outstanding! You have mastered multi-step equations, compound inequalities, absolute value equations and inequalities, and real-world applications. Your Algebra 1 foundation is strong.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review any missed topics: (1) When an equation simplifies to a false statement (like $-12 = 5$), there is no solution, (2) When it simplifies to a true statement (like $-6 = -6$), all real numbers are solutions, (3) For absolute value, $|ax + b| = c$ gives two equations; $|ax + b| < c$ gives one compound inequality; $|ax + b| > c$ gives two separate intervals.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Key areas to review: (1) Clearing fractions by multiplying by the LCD, (2) Recognizing identities (all real numbers) vs. contradictions (no solution), (3) 'And' vs. 'or' in compound inequalities, (4) Absolute value: less than means 'between', greater than means 'outside'.
scoreMessages.0: This review covers essential Algebra 1 equation and inequality skills needed for Algebra 2. Focus on: solving equations with variables on both sides, clearing fractions, identifying special cases (no solution / all real numbers), compound inequalities ('and' = intersection, 'or' = union), absolute value equations (two branches), and absolute value inequalities (less than = between, greater than = outside).
